title: The Opulence of the Absolute
introduction: Chapter 10 of the Bhagavad Gita, Vibhuti Yoga (The Yoga of Divine Opulences), is one of the most awe-inspiring and devotional chapters in the entire scripture. Having revealed His personal nature and the supremacy of devotion in Chapter 9, Krishna now responds to Arjuna’s request to describe His divine manifestations (vibhutis) — the most magnificent, glorious, and powerful expressions of Himself in the universe. This is Krishna’s loving answer to the devotee who longs to see and feel God everywhere. He lists dozens of His supreme opulences — the best, the first, the strongest, the most beautiful, the most essential in every category — so that Arjuna (and every reader) can train the mind to recognize the Divine presence in the grandeur of creation. For modern readers — living in a world of Instagram aesthetics, awe at nature documentaries, scientific wonder at the cosmos, and the search for meaning in everyday life — this chapter is a direct invitation: stop looking for God only in temples or meditation cushions; see Him in the majesty of mountains, the brilliance of the sun, the strength of leaders, the beauty of art, the intelligence of science, the love in relationships. The essence remains perfectly intact: Krishna is not distant — He is the source of all splendor, and by meditating on His opulences, the devotee’s heart becomes saturated with love, wonder, and constant remembrance of the Supreme Person.

shlokas.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
भूय एव महाबाहो शृणु मे परमं वचः |
यत्तेऽहं प्रीयमाणाय वक्ष्यामि हितकाम्यया ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
bhūya eva mahā-bāho śṛṇu me paramaṁ vacaḥ
yat te ’haṁ prīyamāṇāya vakṣyāmi hita-kāmyayā
shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Listen again O mighty armed Arjuna. Because you are My dear friend for your benefit I shall speak to you further giving knowledge that is better than what I have already explained.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna begins by inviting Arjuna to listen once more to His supreme words because Arjuna is His dear friend and Krishna wants only his welfare. This verse opens the chapter on the opulence of the absolute by showing Krishna lovingly sharing deeper divine glories. For people in their thirties who lead teams or families this verse feels like a trusted mentor offering advanced guidance for success. For example a senior executive who has already shared basic strategies now reveals advanced insights to help his protégé excel in the company. Detailed meaning: Krishna reveals that He is about to disclose His unlimited opulences and mystic powers so that Arjuna and all readers can understand the true glory of the absolute. The verse makes readers eager to receive this royal knowledge of divine manifestations. Practice: approach every new lesson from the divine with an open heart so you receive the highest benefit.

shlokas.sanskrit:
न मे विदुः सुरगणा महर्षयः कुतोऽन्ये |
अहमादिर्हि देवानां महर्षीणां च सर्वशः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
na me viduḥ sura-gaṇā maharṣayaḥ kuto ’nye
aham ādir hi devānāṁ maharṣīṇāṁ ca sarvaśaḥ
shlokas.translation: Neither the hosts of demigods nor the great sages know My origin or opulences for in every respect I am the source of the demigods and the great sages.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna declares that even the demigods and great sages do not know His origin or full opulences because He is the original source of all of them. This verse reveals the infinite glory of the absolute in the chapter on divine opulences. For people in their thirties who admire successful leaders this verse shows true ultimate leadership. For example a founder who created an entire industry is the source that even top executives cannot fully trace. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that His glories are beyond ordinary understanding yet He is the root of all existence. This establishes His supreme position. The verse makes readers eager to know more about these divine manifestations. Practice: recognize the divine as the source of all success so your own achievements stay humble and connected.

shlokas.sanskrit:
यो मामजमनादिं च वेत्ति लोकमहेश्वरम् |
असंमूढः स मर्त्येषु सर्वपापैः प्रमुच्यते ||

shlokas.translation: He who knows Me as the unborn as the beginningless as the Supreme Lord of all the worlds he only undeluded among men is freed from all sins.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that anyone who knows Him as unborn beginningless and the Supreme Lord of all worlds becomes free from all sins and delusions. This verse highlights the liberating power of knowing the opulence of the absolute. For people in their thirties seeking freedom from past mistakes this verse offers complete release. For example a leader who understands the divine as the eternal source feels liberated from guilt and moves forward with clarity. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that correct knowledge of His eternal nature destroys all impurities. This is the direct benefit of realizing divine glories. The verse makes readers eager to gain this understanding. Practice: meditate on the divine as eternal and supreme so all burdens dissolve.

shlokas.sanskrit:
बुद्धिर्ज्ञानमसंमोहः क्षमा सत्यं दमः शमः |

shlokas.transliteration:
buddhir jñānam asaṁmohaḥ kṣamā satyaṁ damaḥ śamaḥ
sukhaṁ duḥkhaṁ bhavo ’bhāvo bhayaṁ cābhayam eva ca
shlokas.translation: Intelligence knowledge freedom from doubt and delusion forgiveness truthfulness control of the senses control of the mind happiness and distress birth death fear fearlessness nonviolence equanimity satisfaction austerity charity fame and infamy all these various qualities of living beings are created by Me alone.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna lists many human qualities such as intelligence knowledge forgiveness truthfulness and even happiness and distress and states that all of them originate from Him alone. This verse displays the opulence of the absolute by showing Krishna as the creator of every quality. For people in their thirties developing leadership skills this verse reveals the divine source of all abilities. For example a manager who realizes that his intelligence and calm come from the divine feels empowered yet humble. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that every positive and challenging trait in living beings flows from His divine energy. This expands the understanding of His glories. The verse makes readers eager to see the divine in daily life. Practice: recognize every quality in yourself and others as coming from the divine so you stay connected to the source.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अहिंसा समता तुष्टिस्तपो दानं यशोऽयशः |

shlokas.sanskrit:
महर्षयः सप्त पूर्वे चत्वारो मनवस्तथा |

shlokas.transliteration:
maharṣayaḥ sapta pūrve catvāro manavas tathā
mad-bhāvā mānasā jātā yeṣāṁ lokā imāḥ prajāḥ
shlokas.translation: The seven great sages and before them the four other great sages and the Manus come from Me born from My mind and all the living beings populating the various planets descend from them.

Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that He is the ultimate progenitor of all great teachers and progenitors. This expands His glory as the source of creation. एतां विभूतिं योगं च मम यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः |

अहं सर्वस्य प्रभवो मत्तः सर्वं प्रवर्तते |
इति मत्वा भजन्ते मां बुधा भावसमन्विताः ||

वसूनां पावकश्चास्मि मेरुः शिखरिणामहम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
rudrāṇāṁ śaṅkaraś cāsmi vitteśo yakṣa-rakṣasām
vasūnāṁ pāvakaś cāsmi meruḥ śikhariṇām aham
shlokas.translation: Of all the Rudras I am Lord Śiva. Of the Yakṣas and Rākṣasas I am the Lord of wealth Kuvera. Of the Vasus I am fire. Of mountains I am Meru.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna says that among the Rudras He is Lord Śiva among the Yakṣas and Rākṣasas the Lord of wealth Kuvera among the Vasus fire and among mountains Meru. This verse reveals the opulence of the absolute by showing Krishna as the chief in powerful forces of nature and wealth. For people in their thirties who handle resources or face challenges this verse brings strength. For example someone who manages finances realizes true wealth comes from Krishna and stays balanced like the steady mountain Meru. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that He is the most prominent in each group of mighty beings or natural elements. This demonstrates His supreme control. The verse makes readers eager to see divine power in nature and prosperity. Practice: when you see fire or a tall mountain remember Krishna as its essence so your respect turns into devotion.

senānīnāṁ ahaṁ skandaḥ sarasām asmi sāgaraḥ

shlokas.sanskrit:
महर्षीणां भृगुरहं गिरामस्म्येकमक्षरम् |
यज्ञानां जपयज्ञोऽस्मि स्थावराणां हिमालयः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
maharṣīṇāṁ bhṛgur ahaṁ girām asmy ekam akṣaram
yajñānāṁ japa-yajño ’smi sthāvarāṇāṁ himālayaḥ
shlokas.translation: Of the great sages I am Bhṛgu. Of vibrations I am the sacred syllable Om. Of sacrifices I am the chanting of holy names. Of immovable things I am the Himālayas.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that among the great sages He is Bhṛgu of vibrations the syllable Om of sacrifices the chanting of holy names and of immovable things the Himalayas. This verse reveals the opulence of the absolute through sacred sound and steady presence. For people in their thirties who practice mindfulness this verse connects chanting to divine power. For example someone who repeats a mantra realizes the vibration Om is Krishna and feels steady like the Himalayas. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that He is the essence of wisdom sound and stable creation. This shows His glory in spiritual practices. The verse makes readers eager to use sacred sound. Practice: chant the holy names or Om and remember Krishna as their source so your practice becomes powerful.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अश्वत्थः सर्ववृक्षाणां देवर्षीणां च नारदः |
गन्धर्वाणां चित्ररथः सिद्धानां कपिलो मुनिः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
aśvatthaḥ sarva-vṛkṣāṇāṁ devarṣīṇāṁ ca nāradaḥ
gandharvāṇāṁ citrarathaḥ siddhānāṁ kapilo muniḥ
shlokas.translation: Of all trees I am the banyan tree. Of the celestial sages I am Nārada. Of the Gandharvas I am Citraratha. Of perfected beings I am Kapila.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna says that among all trees He is the banyan tree among celestial sages Nārada among Gandharvas Citraratha and among perfected beings Kapila. This verse shows the opulence of the absolute in nature and divine teachers. For people in their thirties who value stability and guidance this verse offers roots and wisdom. For example someone who sits under a large tree realizes the banyan is Krishna and feels supported by eternal knowledge. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that He is the most prominent in groups of trees sages musicians and realized souls. This illustrates His presence in beauty and teaching. The verse makes readers eager to honor nature and teachers. Practice: respect large trees or wise people and remember Krishna as their essence so your life gains strength and guidance.

shlokas.sanskrit:
उच्चैःश्रवसमश्वानां विद्धि माममृतोद्भवम् |
ऐरावतं गजेन्द्राणां नराणां च नराधिपम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:

airāvataṁ gajendrāṇāṁ narāṇāṁ ca narādhipam

प्रजनश्चास्मि कन्दर्पः सर्पाणामस्मि वासुकिः ||

पितॄणामर्यमा चास्मि यमः संयमतामहम् ||

shlokas.sanskrit:

मृगाणां च मृगेन्द्रोऽहं वैनतेयश्च पक्षिणाम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:

mṛgāṇāṁ ca mṛgendro ’haṁ vainateyaś ca pakṣiṇām

Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that He is the best devotee the controller of time the king of animals and the carrier of the divine. This shows His glory in every realm. shlokas.sanskrit:

झषाणां मकरश्चास्मि स्रोतसामस्मि जाह्नवी ||
shlokas.transliteration:

The verse makes readers eager to feel the divine in nature and heroism. Practice: when you feel a breeze or see a river remember Krishna as its source so your actions gain purity and power.

shlokas.sanskrit:

Practice: whenever you encounter something magnificent remember it is a tiny reflection of Krishna’s glory so your appreciation becomes devotion.
